title: The Lies We Live By: Tower of Babel
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Lies We Live By - Genesis 11_1-9 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Introduction to the Lies We Live By series
The Tower of Babel: humanity’s self-glorifying project
The lie of building a name for ourselves apart from God
God’s response: confusing language and scattering humanity
Cultural lies rooted in the Babel spirit
summary: Part of a series on cultural and personal lies that Christians face. Expounding Genesis 11:1-9, the Tower of Babel, the sermon addresses the primal lie of self-glorification—building a name for ourselves and making ourselves the center of meaning. God’s judgment at Babel reveals the folly of living by this lie.
transcript:
## Lies We Live By: Introduction

We live in a world saturated with self-centered slogans. Consider these statements that appear across social media, streaming shows, and even Christian content:

*Follow your heart. Trust your instincts. Live your truth. You are enough. You should put yourself first. Authenticity is everything. You only have one life. God just wants you to be happy. You should not judge. You are the boss of you. It is all about love. Believe in yourself. You are perfect just as you are.*

What do all these statements have in common? They are self-centered. Truth, in this worldview, comes from within. They are lies that our culture is pushing — and they are lies found not only in secular culture but in prominent Christian blogs, bestselling books, and evangelical media. They sound positive and life-affirming, but they are unhinging people from truth, reality, and hope.

Over the next few weeks, we will examine these lies and seek to replace them with the truths of Scripture.

## Why So Many Lies?

Why is deception so pervasive? Two root causes: Satan is a liar, and the human heart is deceitful.

> *"You are of your father the devil, and the desires of your father you want to do. He was a murderer from the beginning, and does not stand in the truth, because there is no truth in him. When he speaks a lie, he speaks from his own resources, for he is a liar and the father of it."* (John 8:44)

The devil does not appear ugly and terrifying. He appears attractive. He presents things close to truth, things that appeal to us. Paul writes that he can appear as an angel of light (2 Corinthians 11:14).

And our own hearts compound the problem:

> *"The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked; who can know it?"* (Jeremiah 17:9)

Notice: the heart is not only deceivable but also *deceitful* — a source of deception, not just a victim of it.

## A Culture in Crisis

Contemporary Christianity has embraced many of these lies without blinking. Much of what passes for "deconstruction of faith" today is not really a deconstruction of biblical Christianity — it is a deconstruction of a cultural Christianity that borrows biblical language but is not rooted in Scripture.

People are looking to their own hearts, opinions, preferences, biases, and feelings to guide them through life. We have elevated the advice of authors, influencers, and life coaches who just went through a life-altering experience — divorce, paradigm shift, spiritual "deconstruction" — and write books within months of their crisis, instructing others to discard thousands of years of biblical wisdom. Following such people is like getting on a plane flown by a pilot who just watched a few YouTube videos. The danger is real, and yet we accept it readily.

Depression and anxiety are skyrocketing, especially among younger generations. Secular researchers note that this generation cannot ground itself on anything — they feel they are sinking and shifting at the same time. There is a yearning for something that does not change, and they do not have it. Generations that went before them bear some responsibility for failing to pass on the unchanging truths of Scripture.

> *"I will tell to the generation to come the praises of the Lord, and His strength and His wonderful works."* (Psalm 78:4)

## Building on the Rock

The most freeing and stabilizing thing we can do is to cling to the timeless truths of the Bible. Jesus puts it plainly at the end of the Sermon on the Mount:

> *"Whoever hears these sayings of Mine, and does them, I will liken him to a wise man who built his house on the rock: and the rain descended, the floods came, and the winds blew and beat on that house; and it did not fall, for it was founded on the rock. But everyone who hears these sayings of Mine, and does not do them, will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand: and the rain descended, the floods came, and the winds blew and beat on that house; and it fell. And great was its fall."* (Matthew 7:24–27)

The house on the sand and the house on the rock look the same from the outside. The difference only appears when the storms come.

## The Tower of Babel and Our Moment

Genesis 11:1–9 is remarkably relevant. The builders of Babel declared their purpose: "Come, let us build ourselves a city, and a tower whose top is in the heavens; let us make a name for ourselves, lest we be scattered over the face of the whole earth" (Genesis 11:4). Their building project was not born out of humble pursuit of God's worship. They wanted to make a name for themselves. They were striving for a peace and unity that did not recognize the supremacy of God and his word.

Ironically, the very thing they feared — being scattered — is exactly what happened. God dispersed them because they sought unity on their own terms.

In ancient times, they used brick and mortar. Today we use computers, smartphones, and social media to try to build a unified society apart from the supremacy of God and his word. Instead of a tower reaching to heaven, we have built online platforms that proliferate secular morality, self-worship, discord, and misinformation — with people teaching every sort of falsehood to millions.

Our language is still confused. We talk past each other, define words differently, shun logic, and celebrate all viewpoints as equally valid — except, it seems, the biblical one.

## Words Matter: Linguistic Theft

The lies we live by often work through the theft and redefinition of language. Years ago, *tolerance* meant respecting someone's right to hold an opinion you disagreed with. Today it means affirming and celebrating that opinion with no possibility of correction. Notice: the modern usage is not just a new definition — it is the exact opposite of the original meaning.

Other words that have been radically redefined in the last ten to twenty years: *marriage*, *gender*, *love*, *hate*, *racist*, *justice*, *truth*, *oppression*. When language is unstable, communication breaks down. This phenomenon has a name: **linguistic theft** — purposefully hijacking words, changing their definitions, and using those words as tools of propaganda.

Linguistic theft can stop a conversation in its tracks (accuse someone of "hate speech" and debate ends), cause people to act before thinking, obscure the specifics of a discussion, and present negatives as positives or positives as negatives.

## The Gospel of Self

The lies we have surveyed all have a common starting point: the self. To be authentic, I must belong to myself. To be happy, I must put myself first. To be fulfilled, I must be enough for myself. To be successful, I must control my destiny. The problem is that the self is a faulty foundation — it is broken by sin.

For the next several weeks we will examine these lies one by one and seek to answer from Scripture: what is the truth we are actually to live by?
